## Naive Bayes is a useful simplification, not a claim about reality

Naive Bayes is one of the cleanest probabilistic classifiers in `TEM 0052`. It
turns Bayes' rule into a practical prediction method by making one strong modeling
choice: conditional independence between features within each class.

That assumption is almost never literally true in real data. It is still useful
because it gives students a fast, interpretable baseline and a good way to think
about class probabilities, likelihoods, and decision rules.

## From Bayes' rule to a classifier

For a class label `c` and feature vector `x`, the model compares

$$
P(c \mid x) \propto P(c) P(x \mid c).
$$

The "naive" step is to replace the full joint feature distribution with a product of
class-conditional terms:

$$
P(x \mid c) = \prod_{j=1}^p P(x_j \mid c).
$$

That makes estimation much easier. Instead of modeling every interaction directly,
the workflow estimates one probability model per feature, per class, and combines the
results into a class score.

## Why it works well for text data

Text classification is a natural use case because bag-of-words features are already a
large collection of token counts or indicators. In that setting, naive Bayes can
perform surprisingly well even when the independence assumption is clearly false.

For `TEM 0052`, the important lesson is not that the assumption becomes true. The
important lesson is that a deliberately simple model can still produce a strong
baseline when:

- the feature representation is sensible,
- the class prior is estimated honestly,
- and evaluation is done on held-out data.

## Smoothing and log scores matter

In practice, a classifier like multinomial naive Bayes needs additive smoothing. A
zero probability for one unseen token should not force the entire class score to
collapse to zero.

For the same reason, implementations usually work with log scores rather than raw
probability products. The logs are numerically safer and still preserve the same
ranking of classes.

## What students should notice

Naive Bayes is a good teaching model because it reveals several decisions that are
easy to hide in more complex workflows:

- how the class prior affects the prediction,
- how preprocessing changes the feature space,
- why a model can look strong on accuracy while still making costly false positives,
- and how a simple baseline helps us judge whether a more flexible model is actually better.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students why naive Bayes can work well for spam filtering even though words in a
message are obviously not independent.
:::

## Where it fits in `TEM 0052`

Naive Bayes belongs in the classification block alongside kNN and logistic
regression. It gives the course one explicitly probabilistic classifier before the
tree and ensemble material.

It also pairs naturally with the spam-filtering exercise, where students see how text
vectorization, smoothing, and class imbalance shape the final predictions.

## What to report

When students present a naive-Bayes workflow in `TEM 0052`, they should usually
report:

- the feature representation, such as count vectors or tf-idf features,
- the training/test or cross-validation split,
- the smoothing rule,
- the baseline class balance,
- the confusion matrix together with precision and recall,
- and one short comment on whether the model is useful because it is fast,
  interpretable, or a strong baseline for text classification.
